elements.sent-2411-029: Particle systems are a central object in probability, mathematical physics, statistics, and other areas of mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-030: In physical contexts, they model large systems of identical particles, e.g.\ electrons, photons, etc., at the microscopic scale.
elements.sent-2411-031: In many applications, the admissible configurations consist of up to countably many indistinguishable particles which are not allowed to occupy the same position, and are locally finite.
elements.sent-2411-032: Stochastic dynamics for configurations subject to noise has been a very active area of research, in its own (see e.g.~\cite{AlbKonRoe98,AlbKonRoe98b,Osa96,LzDSSuz21 and references therein); as well as in connection with: random matrices (see e.g.\ the monograph~\cite{AkeBaiDiF15 and references therein), infinite sdes (e.g.~\cite{OsaTan20 and references therein), metric measure geometry~\cite{ErbHue15,LzDSSuz22a , only to mention a few.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Here, we consider a different type of (marked) particle systems, which naturally describe physical systems at the mesoscopic scale.
elements.sent-2411-034: At this scale we postulate that,.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the following,~$n$ is always a non-negative integer, while~$N$ denotes either a non-negative integer or the countable infinity.
elements.sent-2411-036: We consider a stochastic particle system of~$N$ marked points in some ambient space~$M$, identified with their position~$X^i$ and mark~$s_i>0$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Whereas we allow for the total number of particles to be infinite, we assume the system to have finite size, so that ---without loss of generality after normalization--- $\sum_i^Ns_i=1$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The marks~$s_i$ are understood as placeholders for a context-relevant scalar quantity such as, e.g., mass or charge.
elements.sent-2411-039: We will refer to this quantity only as to the mass of the particle, and call any particle system as above massive.
elements.sent-2411-040: We assume the sequence of masses~$s_i$ to be randomly distributed in the ordered $N$-simplex, with some arbitrary law~$\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-041: We further assume each particle to be subject to a noise with volatility the inverse mass carried by the particle, viz. \begin{equation}\label{eq:Free} \diff X^i_t = \diff \mssW^i_{t/s_i}\comma \qquad i\leq N\fstop\end{equation} Here the~$\mssW^i_\bullet$'s are independent instances of a same Markov process~$\mssW_\bullet$, possibly with jumps, as, e.g., a standard Wiener process or an $\alpha$-stable L\'evy process.
elements.sent-2411-042: When the ambient space is `open', particles with small mass tend to escape bounded regions in short time and the total mass of the system might generally dissipate.
elements.sent-2411-043: As to prevent this, we require the total mass of a massive system to be conserved.
elements.sent-2411-044: Precisely, we make the following assumption: \begin{assumption [Setting, cf.\ Ass.~\ref{ass:Setting ] The ambient space~$(M,\T)$ is a locally compact second-countable Hausdorff topological space, endowed with an atomless Borel probability measure~$\nu$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The driving noise~$\mssW_\bullet$ is a $\nu$-reversible irreducible recurrent Hunt process on~$M$ with bounded continuous transition functions and converging exponentially fast to equilibrium.

The trajectory of each particle is represented by a different color, with opacity the mass carried by the particle. As the lightest particle has already explored the whole space, the heavier ones have only explored limited portions of it.}

elements.sent-2411-049: Indeed, when~$\sum_i^\infty s_i=\infty$, the kernel~$\bmssh_\bullet$ may very well be singular with respect to~$\nu^\otym{\infty}$.

elements.sent-2411-057: To each massive system we may associate the corresponding empirical measure, defined via the map \begin{equation}\label{eq:IntroMeasureRep} \EM \colon \seq{s_i,X^i_t}_{i\leq N} \longmapsto \mu_t\eqdef \sum_i^N s_i\, \delta_{X^i_t} \comma \qquad t>0\fstop\end{equation} The process~$\mu_\bullet$ is then a stochastic process on the space~$\msP$ of all Borel probability measures on the ambient space~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We stress that~$\mu_t$ does not display the usual properties of empirical measures of particle systems: in the typical case at hand, the number~$N$ of particles is infinite and the positions~$X^i_t$ form a dense subset of the ambient space for all~$t>0$.
elements.sent-2411-059: In particular,~$\mu_t$ cannot be modelled as a configuration in the sense of, e.g.,~\cite{AlbKonRoe98 , and~$\supp \mu_t= M$ for every~$t>0$.}

elements.sent-2411-073: Since the fundamental work of Y.~Brenier, R.J.~McCann, F.~Otto~\cite{Bre91,BenBre00,McC97,Ott01,McC01 , and many others, suitable infinite-dimensional counterparts on~$\msP_2$ of standard geometric objects have been introduced, including: F.~Otto's gradient~$\boldnabla$ and tangent space~$T_\eta\msP_2$ to~$\msP_2$ at a measure~$\eta$~\cite{Ott01 , J.~Lott's connection~\cite{Lot07 , N.~Gigli's exponential map~\cite{Gig11 , etc. Indeed, it is often said that~$\msP_2$ inherits from~$(M,g)$ a formal `infinite-dimensional Riemannian structure' having~$W_2$ as its `Riemannian' intrinsic distance.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-114: Partially in order to overcome this issue, Dean--Kawasaki and related equations are often considered in the approximation of truncated noise~\cite{CorFis23,DjuKrePer24 , spatially correlated noise~\cite{FehGes24,MarMay22 , additional additive noise~\cite{MarMay22 , etc. In particular: A.~Djurdjevac, H.~Kremp, and N.~Perkowski~\cite{DjuKrePer24 show well-posedness of measure-valued solutions with $L^2$~initial datum for a suitable truncation of the noise; B.~Fehrmann and B.~Gess~\cite{FehGes24 prove well-posedness and construct a robust solution theory for a very large class of Dean--Kawasaki-type equations with non-linear viscosity term and a noise of Stratonovich-type colored in space and white in time; F.~Cornalba and J.~Fischer~\cite{CorFis23,CorFis23b , also cf.~\cite{CorFisIngRai23 , show that the description of hydrodynamics fluctuations via solutions to~\eqref{eq:IntroDKfree is still particularly effective.}}}}}}}}

elements.paragraph-2411-006: Modified massive Arratia flow and coalescing-fragmentating Wasserstein dynamics
elements.sent-2411-158: The necessity of considering the paroxysmal regime arises even in the simplest case when the driving noise~$\mssW$ is a Brownian motion on the unit interval or the real line.
elements.sent-2411-159: This is exemplified by the following two (classes of) natural extensions: \begin{enumerate* [$(a)$] \item Konarovsky's modified massive Arratia flow~\cite{Kon17,KonvRe18 , i.e.\ the extension with sticky boundary conditions, in which colliding particles coalesce after their first collision, and behave as a single particle driven by an independent instance of the noise with volatility the inverse of the total mass of the collided particles; \item the aforementioned Konarovsky--von Renesse coalescing-fragmentating Wasserstein dynamics~\cite{KonvRe17 , a class of extensions with non-trivial boundary conditions, in which colliding particles stick together for a random time and eventually split again in a random way.}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: In fact, in the case of the modified massive Arratia flow, the system is cofinitely degenerate in the sense that the number of particles in the system is finite for every~$t>0$.

elements.sent-2411-178: Two simple examples to which our theory applies are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-179: Consider the standard $d$-dimensional torus~$\mbbT^d\eqdef (\R/\Z)^d$ with the flat metric~$g_{\Flat}$ and the associated intrinsic distance~$\mssd_{\Flat}$.

elements.sent-2411-184: Let us collect here further results in connection with other topics.
elements.sent-2411-185: The configuration space~$\Gamma$ over~$M$ is the space of all non-negative measures on~$M$ integer-valued on each compact subset.
elements.sent-2411-186: Equivalently, its elements are locally finite point clouds in~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-187: As the law of a point process on~$M$ may be regarded as a probability measure~$\mbbQ$ on~$\Gamma$, the random motion of a particle system on~$M$ may be regarded as a stochastic process with values in~$\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-188: Suppose we are given a reference measure~$\mbbQ$ on~$\Gamma$; for example, the law~$\pi$ of a Poisson point process on~$M$ with intensity~$\nu$.
elements.sent-2411-189: It is then a paradigm of the theory that one can lift the driving mechanism ---the Markov generator--- of a single random particle in~$(M,\nu)$ to that of an interacting particle system in~$(\Gamma,\mbbQ)$.
elements.sent-2411-190: Ever since the seminal work of D.~Surgailis~\cite{Sur84 lifting a Markov generator~$\mssL$ on~$M$ to its second quantization~$\diff\Gamma(\mssL)$ on the Poisson space~$(\Gamma,\pi)$, this idea has been particularly fruitful; it has led to effective descriptions of particle systems in a variety of settings: the geometric study of free particle systems with invariant measure~$\pi$ by S.A.~Albeverio, Yu.~G.~Kondratiev, M.~Röckner and coauthors~\cite{AlbKonRoe98,KonLytRoe08, RoeSch99 , as well as interacting particle systems with Gibbs~\cite{AlbKonRoe98b,Osa96 , determinantal/permanental~\cite{Osa13,Osa13b,OsaTan14,ChoParYoo98,OsaTan16 , and general reference measures~\cite{Osa98,MaRoe00,LzDSSuz21 ; the theory of infinite stochastic differential equations (isde) developed by H.~Osada and coauthors,~\cite{Osa11,OsaTan14,KawOsaTan22,OsaTan20 ; the geometric studies~\cite{ErbHue15,LzDSSuz22a,BruSuz21,Suz23 , just to name a few.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: It was eventually shown by Z.-M.~Ma and R\"ockner~\cite{MaRoe00 that one can lift $\nu$-symmetric Markov diffusion operators on~$(M,\nu)$ to Markov diffusion operators on~$(\Gamma,\mbbQ)$ in great generality, and in fact even in the absence of a differential structure on~$M$; also cf.~\cite{LzDSSuz21 .}}
elements.paragraph-2411-008: Lifting Markov generators to spaces of probability measures
elements.sent-2411-192: Theorem~\ref{t:IntroMeasRep provides a complete understanding of the measure representation for massive particle systems.}
elements.sent-2411-193: However, from the point of view of analysis on spaces of probability measures, the particular form of the reference measure~$\mcQ_{\pi,\nu}$ poses a serious restriction.
elements.sent-2411-194: In fact, as soon as we move away from a particle-system point of view, this restriction can be lifted.
elements.sent-2411-195: Indeed, let~$\msP^\pa$ be the space of all purely atomic Borel measures on~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-196: Similarly to the case of~$(\Gamma,\mbbQ)$, we can construct a natural local Dirichlet form on~$L^2(\msP^\pa,\mcQ)$ for some arbitrary $\sigma$-finite reference measures~$\mcQ$ concentrated on~$\msP^\pa$, by lifting a Markov diffusion generator~$\mssL$ on the ambient space~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-197: This includes in particular all normalized completely random measures~$\mcQ$ (without deterministic component) in the sense of Kingman~\cite{Kin67 , see Example~\ref{e:NCRMeasures .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: For every Borel probability measure~$\mcQ$ on~$\msP$ concentrated on~$\msP^\pa$, the operator~\eqref{eq:GeneratorIntro on the algebra of extended cylinder functions~\eqref{eq:HCylinderFEps is densely defined and closable on~$L^2(\mcQ)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Its Friedrichs extension is the generator of a Dirichlet form on~$L^2(\mcQ)$.
elements.sent-2411-200: When~$\mcQ$ is not of the form~$\mcQ_{\pi,\nu}$, the existence of a properly associated Markov process ---i.e.\ the quasi-regularity of the above Dirichlet form--- as well as the identification of such process (if any) currently appear out of reach.
elements.sent-2411-201: At the intersection of control and game theories, stochastic analysis, and calculus of variations, the theory of mean-field models has recently seen a fast development; see, e.g., the monographs~\cite{CarDelLasLio19,CarDel18a,CarDel18b .}
elements.sent-2411-202: In the mean-field regime, control and game problems may be rephrased as partial differential equations on spaces of probability measures; for an account of recent developments in this direction see e.g.~\cite{DelHam22,CecDel22,GanMaySwi21 and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Approaches to well-posedness of such equations include ---among others--- metric gradient flows~\cite{ConKraTon23,ConKraTon23b,ConKraTamTon24 , and regularization by noise, e.g.~\cite{ChoGan17,DelHam22,DelHam24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-204: In particular for this second approach, it is a relevant goal of the theory to construct semigroups acting on functionals on~$\msP$ and displaying good smoothing properties.
elements.sent-2411-205: On the one hand, the strength of the smoothing effect depends on the reach of the noise: a finite-dimensional noise may induce Sobolev-to-continuously-differentiable regularization, as it is the case for the process generated by Y.T.~Chow and W.~Gangbo's partial Laplacian~\cite{ChoGan17 on~$\msP_2(\R^d)$; while an infinite-dimensional noise may induce the stronger bounded-to-Lipschitz regularization, as recently shown by F.~Delarue and W.R.P.~Hammersley for the rearranged stochastic heat equation~\cite{DelHam22,DelHam24 on~$\msP(\R)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: On the other hand, however, the very presence of a smoothing effect is related to the structural properties of the noise, and usually arises in mean-field models in which players are subject to a common noise.
elements.sent-2411-207: By contrast, the measure representation~$\mu_\bullet$ of a free massive system experiences only private noise, in that each particle in the system is subject to its own independent instance of the driving noise~$\mssW$.
elements.sent-2411-208: This begs the following question, already hinted at in~\cite[pp.~2f.]{DelHam22 and~\cite[p.~3]{DelHam24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: In order to see this, let~$\T_\mathrm{conn}$ be any topology on~$\msP$ for which~$\msP$ is connected, e.g.\ the narrow topology, the vague topology, the $L^p$-Wasserstein topology for any~$p\geq 1$, or the norm topology induced by the total variation.
elements.sent-2411-210: Then, there exist (plenty of) bounded Borel functions~$u\colon \msP\to\R$ such that \begin{answer [negative] $\mcH_t u$ is not $\T_\mathrm{conn}$-continuous for any~$t>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Thus, since~$\mcH_\bullet$ fixes the indicator functions of (non-trivial) $\mu_\bullet$-invariant sets,~$\mcH_t\car_A=\car_A$ is continuous for some topology on~$\msP$ if and only if~$A$ is simultaneously open and closed.
elements.sent-2411-212: In particular,~$\mcH_\bullet$ is not bounded-to-Lipschitz regularizing, for any distance for which~$\msP$ is connected.
elements.sent-2411-213: Furthermore, since not only particles experience private noise, but also the strength of the noise is private,~$\mu_\bullet$ is not a stochastic flow; and in the absence of the flow property, any smoothing effect of the semigroup~$\mssH_\bullet$ of~$\mssW$ is not immediately reflected by the semigroup~$\mcH_\bullet$ of~$\mu_\bullet$, not even on invariant sets.
elements.sent-2411-214: Precisely,~$\mu_\bullet$ is not a stochastic flow in the sense of Le~Jan--Raimond~\cite{LeJRai04 , since the family of semigroups~$\mssP^\sym{n _\bullet$ describing the evolution of the largest~$n$ particles of~$\mu_\bullet$ does not satisfy the compatibility condition in~\cite[Dfn.~1.1]{LeJRai04 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: In particular, as a consequence of the characterization in~\cite{LeJRai04 , there exists no stochastic flow of random measurable mappings~$\phi_\bullet\eqdef\seq{\phi_{s,t _{0\leq s\leq t}$, with~$\phi_{s,t}\colon M\to M$, so that~$\mu_t={\phi_{0,t _\pfwd \mu_0$ for~$t>0$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: In layman terms, the `flow'~$\mu_\bullet$ is so `turbulent' that the ---arbitrarily fast--- particles in~$\mu_\bullet$ `tear apart the fabric of space in a non-measurable way'.
elements.sent-2411-217: This is further reflected by the non-linear nature of the operator in the singular drift term of~\eqref{eq:IntroSPDE , which does not depend on the masses of the atoms of~$\mu_\bullet$, but rather only on their locations.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Massive particle systems are heavily constrained by their intrinsic pointwise conservation of mass: as soon as Assumption~\ref{ass:QPPIntro is satisfied, the mass of each particle is constant for all times.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Equivalently, there is no dynamics in the mass component, which is the very reason of the ergodic decomposition in Theorem~\ref{t:IntroMeasRep .}
elements.sent-2411-220: While intrinsic to (the measure representation of) massive particle systems, the lack of ergodicity is ---as discussed above--- undesirable.
elements.sent-2411-221: However, if we again move away from a particle-system point of view, it is not difficult to combine the dynamics of positions in Theorem~\ref{t:Intro1 with natural dynamics on the space of masses, resulting in non-trivial irreducibile dynamics on~$\msP$.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Two especially interesting instances are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-223: Suppose again that~$\mcQ=\DF{\nu}$ is the Dirichlet--Ferguson measure in~\S\ref{sss:WBM .}
elements.sent-2411-224: It is an invariant measure for the Dirichlet--Ferguson diffusion, the Fleming--Viot process~\cite{FleVio79 with parent-indepen\-dent mutation, and the Dawson--Watanabe super-process, e.g.~\cite{Per02 .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Denote by~$\mcE^\FleVio$ the Dirichlet form~\cite{OveRoeSch95 of the Fleming--Viot process, and by~$\mcE^\DawWat$ the one of the Dawson Watanabe process.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Finally, let~$\mcE_{\DF{\nu }$ be the form~\eqref{eq:IntroCheeger with~$\mcQ=\DF{\nu}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: The form \begin{equation}\mcE_{\DF{\nu}} + \mcE^*\end{equation} is closable on the algebra of extended cylinder functions~\eqref{eq:HCylinderFEps .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Its closure is a conservative strongly local Dirichlet form on~$L^2(\DF{\nu )$, quasi-regular for the narrow topology~$\T_\mrmn$ (in fact, also for the weak atomic topology~$\T_\mrma$).}
elements.sent-2411-229: The properly associated Markov process is an irreducible $\T_\mrmn$-diffusion~$\mu_\bullet^*$ with state space~$\msP^\pa$.
elements.sent-2411-230: Besides gaining irreducibility/ergodicity, this construction is interesting because it is again possible to explicitly identify the geometry on~$\msP$ for which~$\mu_\bullet^*$ is the geometric Brownian motion in the sense of Theorem~\ref{t:IntroCheeger .}
elements.sent-2411-231: This geometry is (the~$\msP$-section of), respectively, the Bhattacharya--Kantorovich or the Hellinger--Kant\-orovich (also: Wasserstein--Fisher--Rao) geometry on the space of non-negative measures.
elements.sent-2411-232: We refer the reader to~\cite[\S3.1]{LzDS17+ for a comparison between the forms~$\mcE_{\DF{\nu }$ and~$\mcE^\FleVio$, and to~\cite{LzDSSod24 for the construction of the canonical Dirichlet form of the Hellinger--Kantorovich metric measure geometry on the space of all finite measures (not normalized), in the same spirit as in Theorem~\ref{t:IntroCheeger .}}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-012: Possible extensions: jump noise, multiple species, momenta
elements.sent-2411-233: We conclude this Introduction by listing some further possible generalizations and extensions to be addressed elsewhere.
elements.paragraph-2411-011: The Dean--Kawasaki equation with jump noise
elements.sent-2411-234: From a physical point of view,.
elements.sent-2411-235: Mesoscopic systems manifest a multitude of unusual quantum phenomena from localization to strong sample-to-sample fluctuations [...].\guillemotright \cite[p.~302]{AltSim10 .}
elements.sent-2411-236: In mathematical terms, we expect these quantum phenomena ---a simple one being quantum tunnelling--- to be modelled by a noise~$\mssW$ with non-trivial jump part.
elements.sent-2411-237: Due to our use of the machinery of Hilbert tangent bundles, we are not able to give a rigorous meaning to~\eqref{eq:IntroSPDE when~$\mssL$ generates such a noise~$\mssW$.}
elements.sent-2411-238: However, by analogy with the diffusive case, we expect~\eqref{eq:IntroSPDE to admit solutions given as the measure representation of a free particle system driven by some jump noise~$\mssW$ generated by~$\mssL$, exactly as in the diffusive case.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Such systems and their measure representations exist by Theorems~\ref{t:Intro1 and \ref{t:IntroMeasRep .}}
elements.sent-2411-240: In this work we only consider single-species systems.
elements.sent-2411-241: That is, all the particles are subject to (independent instances of) the same driving noise.
elements.sent-2411-242: This is mostly for simplicity, since our techniques apply as well to the case of multiple (possibly: infinitely many) different species, as readily derived from Theorem~\ref{t:BendikovSaloffCoste .}
elements.paragraph-2411-013: Momenta and other physically relevant quantities
elements.sent-2411-243: Our description of massive particle systems relies on the markings~$s_i$.
elements.sent-2411-244: The interpretation of these markings as the mass of each particle is however merely expedient.
elements.sent-2411-245: Indeed, we may as well replace the markings by any other set of quantities~$q_i$ in some abstract space~$Q$ (e.g.~$\R$,~$\R^d$,~$\mbbS^{d-1}$, etc.), together with a function~$\kappa\colon Q\to \R_+$, and satisfying~$\sum_i \kappa(q_i)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-246: These new markings~$q_i$ may then be used to better describe interactions in a multiple-species system, where particles belonging to different species are still driven by the same noise with volatility~$\kappa(q_i)$, but their interaction takes into account the additional degrees of freedom encoded by the $q_i$'s.
elements.sent-2411-247: The simplest example is that of charged particles: $q_i$ is the (positive or negative) charge of a particle, driven by a noise with strength~$\kappa(q_i)=\abs{q_i}$ simply proportional to the charge's magnitude.
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{ \xymatrix@C=4em{ \txt{Wasserstein-type diffusions~\cite{LzDS17+,KonvRe17,KonvRe18}} \ar@{<=>}_{\txt{ local case}}[d] \txt{form on marked space} \ar@{<=>}[r]^{\txt{ quasi-homeo.}}_{\txt{ Thm.~\ref{t:TransferP}}} \txt{form on probab. measures}\ar@/^1.5pc/[r]^{\txt{ Girsanov transf.}}_{\txt{ Thm.~\ref{t:Girsanov}}} \ar@{<=>}[dr] \txt{interacting system} \ar@{=>}[d] \txt{$\infty$-product form~\cite{BouHir91}} \ar@{<=>}[r]^{\txt{ Thm.~\ref{t:BendikovSaloffCoste}}} \ar@{<=>}[d]_{\acts} \txt{form on $\infty$-product~\cite{AlbDalKon00}} \ar@{<=>}[d]^{\txt{ Thm.~\ref{t:BendikovSaloffCoste}}} \ar[u]^{\txt{ direct integration}}_{\txt{ Thm.~\ref{t:DirInt}}} \txt{Dean--Kawasaki spde~\cite{KonLehvRe19,KonLehvRe19b,KonMue23}} \ar@{<=>}[d]_{\acts} \txt{martingale problem} \ar@{<=>}[l]_{\txt{\quad local case}} \txt{$\infty$-product semigroup~\cite{Gil74}} \ar@{<=>}[r]_{\txt{ Cor.~\ref{c:Gill}}} \txt{$\infty$-product kernel~\cite{BenSaC97}} \ar@{<=>}[r]^{\txt{ Cor.~\ref{c:Wellposedness}}} \txt{free massive system} } } \caption{A diagram of the main results}\label{fig:Diagram}
elements.sent-2411-248: Throughout this work, we shall make extensive use of the theory of Dirichlet forms both in its analytic and its probabilistic aspects.
elements.sent-2411-249: We adhere to the standard terminology and notation in the monographs~\cite{MaRoe92,FukOshTak11 and in~\cite{Kuw98,CheMaRoe94 .}}
elements.sent-2411-250: For ease of exposition and for reference, all the relevant definitions and some standard facts are recalled in the Appendix \S\ref{app:DirichletForms .}
elements.sent-2411-251: We will be interested in several constructions on products of up-to-countably many factors.

elements.subsubsection-2411-030: The geometry on probability measures induced by a Markov diffusion
elements.sent-2411-747: Throughout the previous sections, we linked together objects (forms, semigroups, generators) on infinite-product spaces with objects on spaces of probability measures.
elements.sent-2411-748: The main purpose of this link is to provide a robust way to identify the Markov process~$\mu_\bullet$ properly associated with the Dirichlet form~$\tparen{\widehat\mcE_\pi,\dom{\widehat\mcE_\pi }$.}
elements.sent-2411-749: On the one hand, this identification is much stronger than the abstract one provided by the general theory of Dirichlet forms: it is explicit, and allows us to construct the process from every starting point in~$\msP^\pa$ (as opposed to $\widehat\mcE_\pi$-quasi-every starting point, as in the general theory).
elements.sent-2411-750: On the other hand, the identification comes at the expense of generality: firstly, because we need the driving noise to be ergodic, recurrent and ---foremost--- converging exponentially fast to equilibrium, and secondly because we need the invariant measure~$\mcQ_\pi$ to be of the specific form~$\mcQ_\pi\eqdef \EM_\pfwd\widehat\boldnu_\pi$ in~\eqref{eq:DefGeneratorCylP .}
elements.sent-2411-751: However, if we only focus on Dirichlet-form theory, then we may formulate a far more general version of Theorem~\ref{t:TransferQuasiHomeo , lifting practically any Markov diffusion operator to a generator on~$\msP$.}
elements.sent-2411-752: Let~$(M,\T)$ be a metrizable Lusin topological space endowed with a $\sigma$-finite Borel measure~$\sigma$, and~$\msP$ be the space of all Borel probability measures on~$M$ endowed with its usual narrow topology.. Further let.

elements.sent-2411-757: This is trivially satisfied as soon as~$\mcS$ is a finite measure, since functions in~$\hFC{\msA {0}$ are uniformly bounded.}
elements.sent-2411-758: The second part of assumption~\ref{i:t:GeneralForm:D requires the algebra of all such classes to be sufficiently large.}
elements.sent-2411-759: This requirement is non-trivial and poses great limitations on sets of measures on which~$\mcS$ is concentrated.
elements.sent-2411-760: For instance, if~$\mcS$ is concentrated on atomless measures, then~$\tclass[\mcS]{\hFC{\msA {0 }$ reduces to the zero function.}}
elements.sent-2411-761: However, if~$\mcS$ is a finite measure concentrated on~$\msP^\pa$, then~\ref{i:t:GeneralForm:D holds in light of Proposition~\ref{p:PropertiesCylinder}\ref{i:p:PropertiesCylinder:6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-762: This is relevant in light of the following example.
elements.sent-2411-763: An $\msM$-valued random field is called a random measure.
elements.sent-2411-764: In~\cite{Kin67 J.F.C.~Kingman introduced completely random measures: a random measure~$\eta$ is completely random if, for any finite pairwise disjoint collection~$A_1,\dotsc, A_n\in\A$, the random variables~$\eta A_1, \dotsc, \eta A_n$ are independent.}
elements.sent-2411-765: Provided a completely random measure~$\eta$ is $\sigma$-finite with positive probability in the sense of~\cite[Eqn.~(9)]{Kin67 , Kingman~\cite[Eqn.~(43)]{Kin67 shows that~$\eta=\eta_f+\eta_d+\eta_0$ a.s., where \begin{enumerate* [$(a)$] \item $\eta_f$ is purely atomic with up to countably many fixed (deterministic) atoms with random masses; \item $\eta_d$ is deterministic and purely diffuse; \item the ordinary component $\eta_0$ is purely atomic with random atoms and satisfies therefore~$\eta\set{x =0$ a.s.\ for every fixed~$x\in X$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-768: In this case, all the assumptions in Theorem~\ref{t:GeneralForm are verified for~$\mcQ$ (in place of~$\mcS$) as discussed above.}

elements.sent-2411-784: In this section we give a meaning to the Dean--Kawasaki-type spde~\eqref{eq:IntroSPDE and prove that it is well-posed, i.e.\ that existence and uniqueness of solutions holds, in a suitable sense.}
elements.sent-2411-785: We further identify solutions to~\eqref{eq:IntroSPDE as the empirical measures of free massive particle systems described in the previous section.}
elements.sent-2411-786: We start with a brief review of~\cite{KonLehvRe19 , in which solutions to~\eqref{eq:DK0 are defined as solutions to a suitable martingale problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-787: We then adapt the definition of such martingale problem to our setting, and show that solutions to this new martingale problem coincide with the measure representation of free massive particle systems.
elements.sent-2411-788: Rigorous well-pos\-ed\-ness and triviality results for~\ref{eq:DK were recently achieved by V.V.~Konarovsky, T.~Lehmann, and M.-K.~von Renesse in~\cite{KonLehvRe19, KonLehvRe19b .}}
elements.sent-2411-789: In fact, they classified solutions to~\ref{eq:DK in the strongest possible way and in the very general setting of standard Markov triples satisfying Ricci-curvature lower bounds.}
elements.sent-2411-790: Let us recall the concept of solutions to~\ref{eq:DK in~\cite{KonLehvRe19 , expressed in terms of a corresponding martingale problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-791: We call this the shadow martingale problem in order to distinguish it from the `true' martingale problem on~$\msP$ in Theorem~\ref{t:TransferQuasiHomeo}\ref{i:t:TransferP:6 , as we will explain later on.}
elements.sent-2411-792: Precisely, let~$M$ be a Polish space,~$\nu$ be a Radon measure on~$M$ (not necessarily finite), and~$\msP$ denote the space of all Borel probability measures on~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-793: Further let~$(\mssL,\msA)$ be an essentially self-adjoint diffusion operator on~$L^2(\nu)$ with carr\'e du champ \begin{equation}\mssGamma(f,g)\eqdef \mssL (fg)- f\,\mssL g - g\,\mssL f \comma \qquad f,g\in\msA\fstop\end{equation} Finally, let~$\tparen{\Omega,\msF,\msF_\bullet,\mbbP}$ be a stochastic basis satisfying the usual conditions.
elements.sent-2411-794: It is one main intuition of~\cite{KonLehvRe19 that, choosing~$(\mssL,\msA)$ as the standard Laplacian~$(\Delta,\mcC^\infty)$, then~$\rho_\bullet$ is a solution to~$\martp{\alpha {\Delta}$ if and only if it is a distributional solution to~\ref{eq:DK .}}}
elements.sent-2411-795: As in~\cite{KonLehvRe19 , one may thus consider the analogous martingale problem~$\martp{\alpha {\mssL}$ for a more general operator~$(\mssL,\msA)$ as above, and call a solution~$\rho_\bullet$ to~$\martp{\alpha {\mssL}$ a $\msP$-valued distributional solution to the $\mssL$-driven Dean--Kawasaki equation with~$\IE$-interaction.}}}

elements.subsubsection-2411-034: Measurable co-tangent bundle of a Dirichlet space
elements.sent-2411-850: To define~$\xi$, we need a notion of `tangent bundle' associated to~$M$.

elements.sent-2411-883: In this section, we consider massive systems of pairwise interacting particles.
elements.sent-2411-884: Since we are interested in everywhere dense massive systems, the case of attractive interaction is very delicate, as paroxysmal interaction ---in the form of coagulation phenomena--- may occur at each positive time.
elements.sent-2411-885: An explicit description only by means of Dirichlet-form theory appears therefore out of reach.
elements.sent-2411-886: Thus, we mostly confine ourselves to the case of repulsive interactions, to which our techniques are well-suited.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Girsanov transforms in the measure representation

elements.sent-2411-888: It is therefore natural to assume the existence of a distance function on~$M$ and use it to describe these interactions.

elements.sent-2411-1006: For the reader's convenience, let us collect here some definitions, notations, and facts on the theory of Dirichlet forms and Markov processes.

elements.sent-2411-1034: For an $\mssm$-special standard process \begin{equation}\label{eq:MarkovP} \mssW\eqdef \seq{\Omega,\msF,\msF_\bullet,X_\bullet, \seq{P_x}_{x\in M_\partial},\zeta}\end{equation} with state space~$M_\partial$ and life-time~$\zeta$ set, for every Borel probability measure~$\mu$ on~$M$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:MarkovProbab} P_\mu\eqdef \int_{M_\partial} P_x\diff\mu(x) \fstop\end{equation} We denote the sub-Markovian semigroup transition kernel measures of~$\mssW$ by~$\mssh_\bullet$; see~e.g.~\cite[Thm.~IV.1.20, p.~96]{MaRoe92 .}

elements.sent-2411-1048: A set~$G\subset M$ is $\mssE$-quasi-open if there exists an $\mssE$-nest~$\seq{F_n _n$ so that~$G\cap F_n$ is relatively $\T$-open in~$F_n$ for every~$n\in \N$.}

elements.sent-2411-1051: A property~$(p_x)$ depending on~$x\in M$ holds $\mssE$-quasi-everywhere (in short:~$\mssE$-q.e.) if there exists an $\mssE$-polar set~$N$ so that~$(p_x)$ holds for every~$x\in M \setminus N$.

elements.sent-2411-1093: Let~$(M,g)$ be a complete smooth Riemannian manifold of dimension~$d\geq 1$, possibly with (smooth) boundary.

elements.sent-2411-1146: The author thanks the organizers of these events and he is very grateful to Hiroshi Kawabi, Seichiro Kusuoka, and Fran\c{c ois Delarue for the respective invitations.}

elements.sent-2411-1148: For the objects in~\ref{i:Abstract:1 --\ref{i:Abstract:3 we prove existence and uniqueness of solutions, and several characterizations, on an arbitrary locally compact Polish ambient probability space~$(M,\nu)$ for a $\nu$-symmetric exponentially recurrent Feller driving noise.}}
elements.sent-2411-1149: We further show that each $\nu$-symmetric Markov diffusion generator~$\mssL$ on~$M$ induces in a natural way a geometry on the space of probability measures over~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-1150: We construct and fully characterize a class of stochastic dynamics on the space of Borel probability measures over a general locally compact Polish space, arising as the empirical distribution of a free system of infinitely many independently diffusing particles of unequal mass.
elements.sent-2411-1151: These free massive systems serve as a physically motivated model for mesoscopic phenomena, interpolating between microscopic particle systems and macroscopic continuum limits, and are rigorously formulated using the machinery of Dirichlet forms and stochastic partial differential equations (spdes) on Kantorovich--Rubinstein (Wasserstein) spaces.
elements.sent-2411-1152: Our framework recovers and unifies a wide range of previously studied dynamics ---including the Dirichlet--Ferguson diffusion and Dean--Kawasaki equations with singular drift--- and extends them in several directions.
elements.sent-2411-1153: A main result in our work is the identification of the measure representation of a free massive system as a reversible Markov process properly associated with an explicitly constructed quasi-regular Dirichlet form over a space of probability measures.
elements.sent-2411-1154: We compute its generator and semigroup on a dense algebra of extended cylinder functions, and show that it coincides with the Cheeger energy associated to the Wasserstein geometry whenever the ambient space is a weighted Riemannian manifold.
elements.sent-2411-1155: In this setting, we further identify the process as the Wasserstein Brownian motion, extend known Rademacher-type theorems, and obtain sharp Varadhan-type short-time asymptotics for its heat kernel.
elements.sent-2411-1156: Notably, we prove that the generator of the Dirichlet form is essentially self-adjoint, which yields uniqueness of solutions to the associated martingale problem even in the presence of infinite-dimensional gradient-type noise.
elements.sent-2411-1157: Furthermore, we rigorously interpret the associated spde as a Dean–Kawa\-saki equation with singular drift, driven by vector-valued white noise, and provide a complete characterization of its solutions in terms of the underlying particle dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-1158: This resolves longstanding difficulties concerning well-posedness of such equations and collocates them within a rigorous, unified geometric and analytic framework.
elements.sent-2411-1159: Finally, we also extend the theory to a broad class of interacting systems, including sub-critical Riesz- and Dyson-type singular pairwise mean-field interactions, via Girsanov transforms of the underlying Dirichlet forms.
elements.sent-2411-1160: In fact, we allow for interaction energies defined only via local Sobolev-type regularity in the Dirichlet structure, which need be neither mean-field, nor pairwise, nor smooth.
elements.sent-2411-1161: Our work reveals deep connections between infinite-dimensional geometric analysis, stochastic particle systems, and the intrinsic Riemannian structure of Wasserstein spaces.
elements.sent-2411-1162: The resulting framework blends stochastic analysis, optimal transport, and Dirichlet-form theory on highly singular curved infinite-dimensional spaces, and provides a new foundational approach for the analysis of measure-valued dynamics under minimal geometric or analytic assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-1163: Particle systems are fundamental in probability theory, mathematical physics, statistics, and various other areas of mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-1164: In physical contexts, they model large ensembles of identical particles, such as electrons or photons, at the microscopic scale.
elements.sent-2411-1165: In many applications, the admissible configurations consist of up to countably many indistinguishable particles which cannot occupy the same position and remain locally finite.
elements.sent-2411-1166: The study of stochastic dynamics for these configurations subject to noise has been a very active area of research, in itself (see e.g.~\cite{AlbKonRoe98,AlbKonRoe98b,Osa96,LzDSSuz21 and references therein); as well as in connection with: random matrices (see e.g.\ the monograph~\cite{AkeBaiDiF15 and references therein), infinite sdes (e.g.~\cite{OsaTan20 and references therein), metric measure geometry~\cite{ErbHue15,LzDSSuz22a , among others.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1167: Here, we consider a distinct class of (marked) particle systems that naturally describe physical systems at the mesoscopic scale.
elements.sent-2411-1168: At this scale we postulate that:.
elements.sent-2411-1169: We consider a stochastic particle system of~$N$ marked points in an ambient space~$M$, identified with their position~$X^i$ and mark~$s_i>0$.
elements.sent-2411-1170: While we allow for an infinite number of particles, we assume the system to have finite size, ensuring that ---with no loss of generality, after normalization--- $\sum_i^Ns_i=1$.
elements.sent-2411-1171: The marks~$s_i$ serve as placeholders for a context-relevant scalar quantity, such as mass or charge.
elements.sent-2411-1172: For simplicity, we will refer to this quantity only as to the mass of the particle, and call any particle system as above massive.
elements.sent-2411-1173: We assume the sequence of masses~$s_i$ to be randomly distributed in the ordered $N$-simplex according to some arbitrary law~$\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-1174: Additionally, each particle experiences a noise, with volatility inversely proportional to its mass: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Free} \diff X^i_t = \diff \mssW^i_{t/s_i}\comma \qquad i\leq N\fstop\end{equation} Here,~$\mssW^i_\bullet$ represents independent instances of a same Markov process~$\mssW_\bullet$, possibly with jumps, such as a standard Wiener process or an $\alpha$-stable L\'evy process.
elements.sent-2411-1175: When the ambient space is `open', low-mass particles can rapidly escape every bounded region, leading to potential mass dissipation.
elements.sent-2411-1176: To prevent this, we require the total mass of a massive system to be conserved.
elements.sent-2411-1177: Precisely, we make the following assumption: \begin{assumption [Setting, cf.\ Ass.~\ref{ass:Setting ] The ambient space~$(M,\T)$ is a locally compact second-countable Hausdorff topological space, equipped with an atomless Borel probability measure~$\nu$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1178: The driving noise~$\mssW_\bullet$ is a $\nu$-reversible irreducible recurrent (thus conservative) Hunt process on~$M$ with bounded continuous transition functions and exponential convergence to equilibrium.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale=.5, trim={0 70 0 100}, clip]{n5TorusColor2.pdf} \caption{The trajectory up to time~$1$ of a free massive system consisting of five independent massive Brownian particles on a two-dimensional torus. The trajectory of each particle is represented by a different color, with saturation proportional to the mass carried by the particle. As the lightest particle has already explored the whole space, the heavier particles remain more localized due to lower diffusivity.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-040: Main difficulties I: singularity of product densities
elements.sent-2411-1179: While the case~$N<\infty$ is rather straightforward, the claim in Theorem~\ref{t:Intro1 in the case~$N=\infty$ becomes more nuanced.}
elements.sent-2411-1180: On the one hand, the system's finite size plays a crucial role.
elements.sent-2411-1181: Even if the transition kernel of~$\mssW$ has a continuous bounded density with respect to the invariant measure~$\nu$, this property does not necessarily extend to the transition kernel~$\bmssh_\bullet$ of a solution to~\eqref{eq:Free relative to the product measure~$\nu^\otym{\infty}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1182: Indeed, if~$\sum_i^\infty s_i=\infty$, then~$\bmssh_\bullet$ may be singular with respect to~$\nu^\otym{\infty}$.
elements.sent-2411-1183: This phenomenon is evident even in simple cases such as ~$M=\mbbS^1$, as demonstrated by Ch.~Berg in~\cite{Ber76 in light of S.~Kakutani's characterization~\cite{Kak48 of absolute continuity for infinite-product measures.}}
elements.sent-2411-1184: Notably, if~$\bmssh_\bullet$ is not absolutely continuous with respect to~$\nu^\otym{\infty}$, neither the $L^2$-theory, nor many results in the Feller theory apply.
elements.sent-2411-1185: In the first case this is due to the lack of a natural invariant measure; in the second case since the density of~$\bmssh_\bullet$ (even when it exists) may fail to be continuous.
elements.sent-2411-1186: On the other hand, given that the system has finite size, the sequence~$\seq{s_i _{i}$ is summable, and thus vanishing.}
elements.sent-2411-1187: Consequently, for~$N=\infty$, particles experience arbitrarily large noise strength~$s_i^{-1}$, leading to further complexities in the system’s stochastic behavior such as the absence of natural space-time scaling.
elements.sent-2411-1188: In proving Theorem~\ref{t:Intro1 , we greatly expand on results by T.~Gill, by A.~Bendikov and L.~Saloff-Coste, and by S.A.~Albeverio,~A.Yu.~Daletskii, and Yu.G.~Kondratiev.}
elements.sent-2411-1189: Their respective contributions concern infinite-product strongly continuous contraction semigroups, infinite-product semigroups of heat-kernel measures, and Dirichlet forms on infinite-product spaces.
elements.sent-2411-1190: Specifically (see Thm.~\ref{t:BendikovSaloffCoste below), we achieve the following:.}
elements.sent-2411-1192: We generalize the proof by A.~Bend\-ikov and L.~Saloff-Coste~\cite{BenSaC97 of the absolute continuity of infinite-product heat-kernel measures with respect to infinite-product probability reference measures.}
elements.sent-2411-1193: Their original work was restricted to compact spaces; our extension applies to more general non-compact spaces.
elements.sent-2411-1195: We extend the canonical Dirichlet-form construction on infinite products of compact Riemannian manifolds, originally developed by S.A.~Albeverio, A.Yu.~Daletskii, and Yu.G.~Kondratiev~\cite{AlbDalKon97,AlbDalKon00 , to non-local, non-smooth, and non-compact settings.}
elements.sent-2411-1196: Identifying correspondences between semigroups and forms.
elements.sent-2411-1197: We establish that the semigroup of infinite-product heat-kernel measures from~\ref{i:BSCADK:1 corresponds precisely to the Dirichlet form constructed in~\ref{i:BSCADK:2 , and that the latter is in turn isomorphic to an infinite-product Dirichlet form in the sense of~\cite[\S{V.2.2 ]{BouHir91 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1199: We further show that the infinite-product heat-kernel measure in~\ref{i:BSCADK:1 is the integral kernel of the infinite-tensor-product semigroup on a specific von Neumann-incomplete infinite tensor product of the corresponding $L^2$-spaces, as constructed by T.~Gill in~\cite{Gil78, Gil74 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1200: For a visual overview of our main results, see Figure~\ref{fig:Diagram .}
elements.sent-2411-1201: Each massive system can be associated with an empirical measure, defined by the map \begin{equation}\label{eq:IntroMeasureRep} \EM \colon \tseq{(s_i,X^i_t)}_{i\leq N} \longmapsto \mu_t\eqdef \sum_i^N s_i\, \delta_{X^i_t} \comma \qquad t>0\fstop\end{equation} The process~$\mu_\bullet$ is then a stochastic process taking values in the space~$\msP$ of all Borel probability measures on the ambient space~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-1202: We emphasize that, generally,~$\mu_t$ does not display the usual properties of empirical measures of particle systems.

elements.subsubsection-2411-050: Breaking local conservation of mass and gaining ergodicity
elements.sent-2411-1315: Massive particle systems are heavily constrained by their intrinsic pointwise conservation of mass: as soon as Assumption~\ref{ass:QPP is satisfied, the mass of each particle is constant for all times.}
elements.sent-2411-1316: Equivalently, there is no dynamics in the mass component, which is the very reason of the ergodic decomposition in Theorem~\ref{t:TransferP}\ref{i:t:TransferP:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-1317: While intrinsic to (the measure representation of) massive particle systems, the lack of ergodicity is undesirable.
elements.sent-2411-1318: However, it would not be difficult to combine the dynamics of positions in Theorem~\ref{t:BendikovSaloffCoste with natural dynamics on the space of masses, resulting in non-trivial irreducibile dynamics on~$\msP$.}
elements.sent-2411-1319: Indeed, the Dirichlet form $\tparen{\mcE_\pi,\dom{\mcE_\pi }$ in Theorem~\ref{t:Wasserstein can be combined with other known Dirichlet forms on~$\msP$.}}
